item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: how does that last line manage to get me every single time i read this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nowadays not the only car in space!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: PlEaSe LeT tHiS bE a NoRmAl FiElD tRiP

item.title: Re: Hey guys
item.content:encoded: I loved your reading of this unforgettable SCP. I know that was years ago, but time is of little relevance to the foundation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Me too Man. Far out!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Am I the only one that’s been crying after reading this

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This started out as ridiculous and somewhat funny. Then the log came around, and… holy crap… that last line… I think there's something in my eye.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my headcannon Jerry had some secondary device he was gonna activate once they got a certain distance from Earth that Will didn't know about, only he had that accident and died before it was time. No one, absolutely no one, with the ability to read star charts is going to think you can get to Alpha Centari at the speed they were going. That would strain believability far too much for me, but the idea that the guy who shoved all the extra tech into the engine compartment had a plan that he died before he could put into effect makes a lot more sense.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love scips were stupid people suffer because of their own stupidity. But I dislike when people-that-are-actually-really-smart-and-have-access-to-impossible-technology make dumb oversights or suffer really strong lapses in logic. That's what puts me off in Prometheus Labs stuff, and also here. But this scip is so good as to get over that distaste. So I'll upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Judging from the clean environment in the car it shouldn't be a skeleton but more akin to a mummy.

Also, they got really high. Ba da Bing.

item.title: 'University of California, San Francisco' is inaccurate
item.content:encoded: UCSF was always a research university, and was known as 'the Medical Department of the University of California' from 1873 to 1964. It's unlikely that an undergrad would be taking classes there.

item.title: Re: Lacking Space Suits
item.content:encoded: My assumption is that because they were hippies and probably none of the work for a space agency, they only decided to bring one suit. When the person wearing it died, no one could go outside to retrieve it and so it was lost in space

item.title: Why is it still there?
item.content:encoded:
See, I love this, but I still can't see why on Earth Mars it's still in space. Sure, it's Safe-class and "self-contained" by its location, but "acquisition and recovery of SCP-1958 itself is pending development of a suitable transport device" just doesn't work for me when it's been boarded by Foundation personnel and established to be "driveable." At the very least they'd consider pointing it back towards the moon base (after all, the further it gets the harder it'll be to recover), and there's no explanation of why they didn't just put it in neutral and tow it (yeah, ∆v budget, but it's not that heavy and not budgeting for a tow would have been a major oversight if it turned out not to be safe). Heck, even pointing it at Jupiter would get rid of it in ~600 years if it's not recovered, rather than the thousands it'll take for it to exit our visible neighborhood.

I know it's nitpicky, but it nagged at me right through the end. Even a brief line saying they couldn't budget for a tow, it turned out to have greater mass than expected, it was anomalously difficult to move (due to whatever propulsion system it's using), or that they didn't want to turn it around because it would be more visible on the way back to the moon would have made a big difference.

item.title: Lacking Space Suits
item.content:encoded:
The diary makes reference to space suits, and only one is used up. Since it doesn't have any reason to not have one aboard, a hippie space suit could be among the retrieved objects. It could even be an SCP by itself, though it isn't at all necessary.

I also think the question about the Moon being nearby could be easily solved, or at least become more plausible, with two little changes: the Moon could be visible for some moments (due relative movement of both objects) through the mirror, like Earth was before. And the starting velocity could be greater than the current, since it was just out of escaping velocity.

Currently the engine of the thing is mostly to compensate for gravitational disruptions in the set route. It would also help, with the notion that the anomalous motion serves to keep trajectory without so much natural deviation, without need for the amount of calculation needed for such a trip. It would even make you imagine that the trip was doomed in the moment they tried to rescue their leader and first dead, fucking with his calculations and the speed of the trip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Started out as funny..

Then it got depressing. Good work man, have a pat on the back.

+1
I just four hundreded you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You'd think The Foundation would want to retrieve it for study, or perhaps they got enough info from observing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Um, they used new age religion to give a VW bus a pseudovelocity drive with launch, transorbital and presumably unused relativistic capabilities. That's fairly well anomalous, isn't it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: While I really like the narrative this creates, it feels like it either should already have an EX status or would get one in the near future. That it lacks one and doesn't really explain what's "missing" to make it still anomalous is keeping me a non-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's going the distance, He's going for speed

item.title: Re: Can anyone explain this to me?
item.content:encoded: Hmm. Good point. It has to be the Moon he sees to clue him in that they've screwed up, because they're not going fast enough to have made it anywhere else yet, but it's a little implausible that he'd only just be noticing it. Perhaps the trajectory they were on kept the Moon out of sight for awhile? I'm no astronomer. Chalk it up to artistic license.

item.title: Can anyone explain this to me?
item.content:encoded:
I like this SCP. I really do. And despite the following discrepancy, I upvoted.

"7/4/5█: …We lit a couple up and said goodbye to Mother Earth for one last time."

"9/18/5█: I can see the Moon out the side window."

So this William guy is distressed to see the Moon after a little over two months have gone by, as this means they're literally still light years away from their intended destination. But if the crew spent this whole time traversing the distance between the Earth and the Moon, the Earth would have been in full view of the microbus. The entire time. If you're in the proximity of the Moon's orbit, Earth still looks huge compared to everything else in the distance. It would have been obvious to them that they were barely making any headway if they had been regularly looking out the windows.

Is there something I'm failing to grasp? I was thinking that maybe they went really far away from the Earth-Moon system and somehow ended up circling back to it. But they're supposed to be on a straight shot trajectory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck, it's Into The Wild in space. Poor dumb bastards

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly what I thought. :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's deep, man. Upvoted.

Congrats Smapti on 300 upvotes on this little beauty!

I think this is relevant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Would an '50s VW van have a dashboard clock? I assume so, but that is merely an assumption (I don't want to make an ass out of muptions, after all).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would imagine so much would have been included among the artifacts found on-site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: With some sort of clock, most likely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How was William ███████ able to keep track of the date?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: By driving, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How the HELL did four hippies manage to get a van into space, and then think they'd get to Alpha Centauri in 4 weeks going 80 miles per hour???

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I found myself chuckling and grinning halfway though, and than feeling mildy scummy near the end of the article for finding their plight amusing.

Upvoted for being funny and dark *sequentially*.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't liking this, right up till the diary, and then it hit me in the feels. Nicely done.

item.title: Hey guys
item.content:encoded:
I posted this video like half a year ago not expecting it to ever be heard, but was surprised by the praise it received! Thought I would post it here. I transcribed William's diary as audio recordings, and here are the results! Hope you like ^^

http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=vOXOTabrxDk

item.title: Re: First +200 article
item.content:encoded:
Oi, this was YOUR doing? Smapti, you're even cooler than I thought you were.

This article is a great example of how to tell a story without breaking tone.

item.title: Re: First +200 article
item.content:encoded: Grats, well deserved =D

item.title: First +200 article
item.content:encoded: Thank you everyone for your support.

item.title: Re: Scurvy?
item.content:encoded: I went with scurvy in order to highlight the tragic naivete of the crew. Jerry knew enough about space to know that radiation would be a problem and shielded the van appropriately, but it hadn't occurred to him that they'd start having health problems if they weren't getting enough vitamin C. (They were probably living off of Spam and canned beans and such.)

item.title: Scurvy?
item.content:encoded: Scurvy? Why not radiation poisoning? Somehow that would seem more appropriate for the story…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rather enjoyed this, and as Nighkos said, the diary is a gem. There is one small detail that gets to me, and it's only because I restore VW's for a living. On the Type-2's, the only lines visible on the undercarriage would be the flexible brake hoses to the wheels. The fuel lines and hard hydraulics actually travel inside the floor pan structure. As far as any sort of coolant line goes, it was an air cooled engine, so there would be no such thing… That said, if this were something retrofitted for space travel or for life support/climate control, it still makes sense. My question then would be: At quick visual inspection of SCP-1958 by the recovery team, would the (D-class?) personnel be able to deduce the function of the severed line?

ftfy- Scantron

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The SCP was pretty good on its own, but the diary really makes this one stand out. I can't help but wondering how extra-terrestrial life will react when it discovers this thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This blurs the Tale/SCP line, but I still like it. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Stop not being dead!

Or don't, I'm a grading machine, not a cop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I cannot upvote this fast enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heh heh, like the fact they brought a Jack Proton book and Scientology literature.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This may be one of the dumbest ideas for an SCP I've ever heard outside of my own or a -J…

…and you SO COMPLETELY made it work.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This SCP is far out. Really, the execution is just stellar.
Seriously though, +1 for triggering emotions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've read this one over and over and every single time it makes me cry. It vaulted to the top of my favorites list in my first read. Totally brilliant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The journals work so well with the description.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When asked to explain to my family why I spend all my spare time reading these things, I will just send them the link to this article. This is delightful.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why do I get the feeling there should be 2 men, 2 women and a huge-ass dog instead of a cat?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This seemed fitting.
http://forlackofabettercomic.com/?id=194

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Observing a hippies progress from idealistic to disillusioned is never a pleasant thing, but well done.

On the comparisons to 907, yes, they both involve a microbus IN SPAAAACE, but that's really where it ends, and to my mind, that's setting, not story. (Commas FTW) All the stuff that actually matters is completely different.

Anyway, +1. I don't love it for the same reasons that I love 907s Mystery Machine, but I do love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This song feels relevant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Smapti, you have once more played pinball with my emotions.

How do you keep taking mediocre-to-bad concepts and making them gold? You have a Philosopher's Stone, don't you? Don't you!?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the best SCP articles I've read recently. Good work, Smapti.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the fifthist church works well in a parodic context. In the SCP universe Fifthism is our "crazy cult", and these are SCPF hippies that used to be involved in one. Seems very natural.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Never liked the Fifthist concept. This would be stronger without it.

Loved everything else, though. +1
